titleStmt.title: Letter from James Finn to May Fay, 16 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Finn

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from James Finn (1877-1922) to May Fay (1897-1976) expressing joy at her her letter confirming her affection for him and their engagement. James also advises May to warn her brother about news he has heard in relation to the beef market and regrets that she will not be able to visit him in Dublin as soon as he hoped.James Finn was a civil servant, working in the National Health Service Commission on Mount Street. While neither James nor May were politically active their letters provide a very unique insight into the affairs of Easter Week and its aftermath. James died in 1922 as a result of the flu epidemic, May was pregnant with their fourth child at the time.May Fay became engaged to James Finn in January 1916. Despite the twenty year age gap their letters show a very personal and private insight into courting in early 20th century Ireland. She held on to his letters as her reminder of him. May never remarried and focused her attention instead on her businesses in order to provide for their children.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
39, Belgrave Square, Rathmines, Dublin. 16th Jan, 1916. My dear, dear girl, This will have to be a
short letter if I am to catch the
post so I hope you will forgive me.
I need hardly tell you how delighted
I was to have your dear letter
and to see by it that you have a
real affection for me. I know that
I don't deserve it and I keep
wondering all the time what I have
ever done to deserve such a blessing.
I suppose good intentions sometimes
earn a reward such as your love
is and I know anyway that my
present intentions are sincere and
good. I hope, however badly the
execution may turn out, with
you to help me perhaps it may not
be so bad as I sometimes fear. Well, my dearest, I was sorry
to see you wonât be able to
come up as soon as I had hoped
but I must only have patience till I
see you. Iâm sorry to hear that Jennie is knocked up but of course it
is nothing serious. Itâs a good thing your
mother has decided to go down and
see her for Iâm sure she wouldnât
be contented till she saw her
mother. They will have a lot to say
to each other so perhaps she may not
come back as soon as I hope. However
thereâs no use in my being selfish anyhow;
I ought to be more than thankful with
things as they are and I am too. Tell Christy and Tommy Seery that
a friend of mine heard English cattle
buyers talking at Dublin market on
Thursday and saying that beef will be
1/6 a lb on the beastâs back before a month.
So they had better stiffen their prices for
any stall feds they have remaining.
From all I hear everything points to very
high prices for cattle in the near future.
So theyâd better avail of the tip. My mother was delighted to have
such a very nice letter from Mrs Fay
this morning. She let me see it and I
must say I was honestly amused with
her good opinion of this miserable sinner.
I can hardly help laughing every time I hear
people gassing about my goodness Hope this catches the post so must
shut up with my heartâs warmest love to the
best little girl in the world. Please donât laugh. Itâs
true. Yours ever fondly, James
